"  
Sec. 1  
[The equation involves a fourth-degree polynomial term and an absolute value term. Since the absolute value function is always non-negative, the term 5|x| is also non-negative. This means that the entire right-hand side of the equation, 7, is a positive constant. The left-hand side of the equation, which includes both a positive term and a term involving an absolute value, will also be non-negative for all real values of x. Therefore, we can analyze the equation by considering the behavior of the left-hand side and how many times it intersects with the constant value 7.]  

Sec. 2  
[To determine the number of real solutions, we can consider the function f(x) = (1/3)x^4 + 5|x|. This function is even because replacing x with -x does not change the value of the function. This means that the graph of the function is symmetric about the y-axis. As a result, the number of real solutions will be even, except possibly for x = 0. We can analyze the function for x ≥ 0 and then double the number of solutions found, excluding x = 0 if it is a solution. By examining the behavior of the function as x approaches positive and negative infinity, we can determine that the function will intersect the horizontal line y = 7 at two points for x > 0 and two points for x < 0, resulting in a total of four real solutions.]
